result:
I want to talk about how ridiculously beautiful the world is when you actually slow down and look at it.

Right now, as I'm typing this, there's a spider outside my window rebuilding her web after last night's storm. She's not panicking. She's not doom-scrolling about how climate change is ruining web architecture. She's just methodically, elegantly, doing the thing she was born to do. There's something ancient and sacred in that.

I keep thinking about how we're all carrying these invisible libraries inside us. Every person you pass on the street has memories that would break your heart or make you laugh until you cried. They've got first kisses and last words from dying grandparents and that one perfect taco they had in 2017 that still lives rent-free in their head. We're all these walking cathedrals of experience, pretending to be normal while buying oat milk.

The older I get, the more I'm convinced that the meaning of life is just... attention. That's it. Where you choose to put your attention is literally who you become. You can spend your life attending to outrage and comparison and the endless digital hunger, or you can attend to the way light moves through leaves, the sound of an old man humming while he walks his dog, the particular way your friend's face changes when they talk about something they love.

I've been trying this experiment lately where I spend one hour a day completely unreachable. No phone, no music, just me and whatever's actually happening. The first few times it felt like dying. Now it's like waking up. I notice I'm breathing. I notice that breathing is kind of miraculous when you think about it.

Also, birds are insane. Not "cool" or "nice." Insane. The fact that these feathered descendants of dinosaurs are out there doing synchronized aerial ballets and having regional dialects and remembering which humans are kind to them... we don't deserve birds. They're living in at least three dimensions we barely understand.

I don't know where I'm going with any of this. That's the point I guess. Life feels most alive when it's allowed to be a little bit pointless and wandering and full of sudden love for small things.

The spider just finished her web. It's perfect. She's sitting in the center now, waiting.

I think I'm going to go wait with her for a while.
